items.subtopic: Cough | COUGH MECHANISM
items.title: Mechanisms Initiating Cough Reflex
items.content: The cough reflex is initiated by both chemical and mechanical stimuli. Chemical stimuli such as capsaicin activate cationic channels like transient receptor potential channels, while mechanical stimuli involve mucus or particulates in air pollution activating adenosine triphosphate-activated ion channels (P2X3). These sensory neuronal receptors transmit signals centrally via Aδ fibers for mechanosensory input and C fibers for chemosensory input.
keyPoints: Chemical stimuli include capsaicin, which activates cationic channels.
Mechanical stimuli involve mucus or particulates in air pollution.
Sensory signals are transmitted via Aδ (mechanosensory) and C fibers (chemosensory).
Afferent nerve endings innervate the pharynx, larynx, airways to terminal bronchioles, lung parenchyma, external auditory canal, and esophagus.
items.reference: Cough · Cough | COUGH MECHANISM

items.subtopic: Cough | COUGH MECHANISM
items.title: Efferent Limb of Cough Reflex
items.content: The efferent limb of the cough reflex involves a series of involuntary muscular actions. The vocal cords adduct, causing transient upper-airway occlusion. Expiratory muscles contract to generate positive intrathoracic pressures up to 300 mmHg. Upon sudden release of laryngeal contraction, rapid expiratory flows are produced, exceeding normal maximal expiratory flow rates. Bronchial smooth-muscle contraction and dynamic compression narrow airway lumens, maximizing exhalation velocity. The kinetic energy for dislodging mucus is proportional to the square of the expiratory airflow velocity.
keyPoints: Vocal cords adduct causing transient upper-airway occlusion.
Expiratory muscles contract generating pressures up to 300 mmHg.
Rapid expiratory flows exceed normal maximal expiratory flow rates.
Bronchial smooth-muscle contraction and dynamic compression narrow airways.
Kinetic energy for dislodging mucus is proportional to the square of expiratory airflow velocity.
items.reference: Cough · Cough | COUGH MECHANISM

items.subtopic: TABLE 40-1 Causes of Impaired Cough and Airway Clearance | IMPAIRED COUGH
items.title: Understanding the Mechanisms Behind Impaired Cough
items.content: Impaired cough can result from various factors including respiratory muscle weakness, chest wall deformities, central respiratory depression, abnormal airway secretions, ciliary dysfunction, and structural abnormalities. These conditions compromise the ability to clear lower respiratory tract secretions, increasing susceptibility to infections.
keyPoints: Respiratory muscle weakness and pain are common causes of impaired cough.
Cough strength can be assessed using peak expiratory flow or maximal expiratory pressure at the mouth as surrogate markers.
Assistive devices range from simple techniques like splinting with a pillow to complex mechanical cough-assist devices.
items.reference: Cough · TABLE 40-1 Causes of Impaired Cough and Airway Clearance | IMPAIRED COUGH | SYMPTOMATIC COUGH

items.subtopic: TABLE 40-1 Causes of Impaired Cough and Airway Clearance | SYMPTOMATIC COUGH
items.title: Differential Diagnosis Based on Duration and Associated Symptoms of Cough
items.content: The duration and associated symptoms of cough can guide differential diagnosis. Acute cough (<3 weeks) often indicates respiratory tract infection or inhalation issues, subacute cough (3–8 weeks) may follow tracheobronchitis, while chronic cough (>8 weeks) suggests a range of cardiopulmonary diseases or conditions like asthma and gastroesophageal reflux.
keyPoints: Acute cough is typically due to respiratory infections or inhalation issues.
Subacute cough often results from pertussis or postviral tussive syndrome.
Chronic cough (>8 weeks) may be caused by asthma, GERD, rhinosinusitis, ACE inhibitors, or chronic bronchitis in smokers.
items.reference: Cough · TABLE 40-1 Causes of Impaired Cough and Airway Clearance | IMPAIRED COUGH | SYMPTOMATIC COUGH

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF CHRONIC COUGH
items.title: When to Order Imaging for Chronic Cough
items.content: A chest radiograph is warranted in virtually all cases of chronic cough evaluation, as it can reveal serious underlying conditions such as sarcoidosis, Hodgkin's disease, lung cancer, or pulmonary tuberculosis.
keyPoints: Chest radiograph recommended for nearly all chronic cough evaluations.
Identifies serious illnesses like sarcoidosis, Hodgkin's disease in young adults, lung cancer in older patients, and pulmonary tuberculosis.
items.reference: Cough · ASSESSMENT OF CHRONIC COUGH

items.subtopic: ASSESSMENT OF CHRONIC COUGH
items.title: Evaluating Sputum in Chronic Cough
items.content: In cases of chronic productive cough, expectorated sputum should be examined to determine the cause of mucus hypersecretion. Purulent-appearing sputum requires bacterial culture and possibly mycobacterial culture, while cytologic examination can help assess for malignancy or distinguish between neutrophilic and eosinophilic bronchitis.
keyPoints: Examination of expectorated sputum is crucial in chronic productive cough.
Purulent-appearing sputum should undergo routine bacterial culture and, if indicated, mycobacterial culture.
Cytologic examination can help assess for malignancy and differentiate between neutrophilic and eosinophilic bronchitis.
items.reference: Cough · ASSESSMENT OF CHRONIC COUGH

items.subtopic: CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 1)
items.title: ACE Inhibitor-Induced Cough: Mechanism and Management
items.content: ACE inhibitor-induced cough occurs in 5–30% of patients due to the accumulation of bradykinin, which sensitizes sensory nerve endings. A trial period off the medication is recommended for patients with chronic unexplained cough on ACE inhibitors.
keyPoints: Occurs in 5–30% of patients taking ACE inhibitors.
Not dose-dependent; involves accumulation of bradykinin.
Trial period off medication to observe decrease in cough.
Angiotensin receptor blockers do not cause cough.
items.reference: Cough · CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 1)
items.title: Cough Hypersensitivity Syndrome: Recognition and Diagnosis
items.content: A distinct 'cough hypersensitivity syndrome' is characterized by a dry cough, worsened by talking or exertion, with no specific diagnostic criteria. It is diagnosed when other etiologies are excluded.
keyPoints: Characterized by dry cough, sensitivity in throat, worse with talking/exertion.
More common in women than men.
Diagnosis suspected after exclusion of alternative causes.
items.reference: Cough · CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 1)
items.title: Cough-Variant Asthma: Identification and Testing
items.content: Cough-variant asthma, common in children but not adults, is identified by cough linked to asthma triggers without wheezing. Diagnosis can be confirmed with spirometry or bronchoprovocation tests.
keyPoints: Common among children; rare in adults.
Diagnosis involves linking cough to asthma triggers.
Spirometry shows airflow obstruction that varies or reverses.
Bronchoprovocation challenge (e.g., methacholine) can confirm/exclude diagnosis.
items.reference: Cough · CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 1)

items.subtopic: CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 2)
items.title: Eosinophilic Bronchitis: Diagnosis and Treatment
items.content: Eosinophilic bronchitis is an uncommon cause of chronic cough with a normal chest radiograph, characterized by sputum eosinophilia greater than 3% without airflow obstruction or bronchial hyperresponsiveness. It responds well to inhaled glucocorticoids.
keyPoints: Sputum eosinophilia > 3% indicates eosinophilic bronchitis
No airflow obstruction or bronchial hyperresponsiveness present
Inhaled glucocorticoids are effective treatment
Elevated exhaled nitric oxide can detect eosinophilic airway inflammation and predict response to inhaled steroids
items.reference: Cough · CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 2)

items.subtopic: CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 2)
items.title: Empirical Treatment of Chronic Cough with Normal Chest Radiograph
items.content: Treatment for chronic cough when the chest radiograph is normal often involves empirical therapy targeting likely causes based on history and physical examination. This may include addressing postnasal drainage, gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), or cough-variant asthma.
keyPoints: Empirical treatment targets most likely cause(s) of chronic cough
Postnasal drainage treatments: systemic antihistamines, decongestants, antibiotics, nasal saline irrigation, and nasal sprays with glucocorticoids, antihistamines, or anticholinergics
GERD management includes antacids, H2 receptor antagonists, proton pump inhibitors, dietary changes, head elevation during sleep, and medications like alginates
Cough-variant asthma responds to inhaled glucocorticoids and intermittent inhaled beta-agonist bronchodilators
items.reference: Cough · CHRONIC COUGH WITH A NORMAL CHEST RADIOGRAPH (part 2)

items.subtopic: Cough | COUGH MECHANISM
items.question: Which type of nerve fibers are primarily involved in transmitting chemosensory signals for cough initiation?
items.explanation: Chemosensory signals for cough initiation are transmitted via C fibers.

items.subtopic: Cough | COUGH MECHANISM
items.question: What is the effect of a deep breath preceding a cough on expiratory muscles?
items.explanation: A deep breath preceding a cough optimizes the function of the expiratory muscles.
